description:
You are a DevSecOps engineer who must turn a CSV-based service inventory into a JSON “policy-as-code” artifact and an accompanying compliance log.

Initial state
1. A directory tree has already been created for you:
   • /home/user/devsecops/policy_input/  
   • /home/user/devsecops/policy_output/  
   • /home/user/devsecops/logs/

2. Inside /home/user/devsecops/policy_input/ there is a file named services.csv containing five (5) service records.  
   • The file is UTF-8 text.  
   • Column order is exactly: service,port,protocol,approved (comma-separated, header in the first line).  
   • The approved column contains the strings yes or no.

Your tasks
A. Read /home/user/devsecops/policy_input/services.csv and build a JSON policy file named
   /home/user/devsecops/policy_output/allowed_ports.json

   • The JSON must contain a single top-level object with one key, allowed_ports.  
   • allowed_ports has an array value.  
   • The array lists every port (as an integer) whose approved column equals the string yes.  
   • The array must appear in ascending numeric order.  
   • Whitespace inside the JSON does not matter, but the structural characters must be valid JSON.  
   • Example shape (DO NOT copy numbers):  
     {
       "allowed_ports": [22,443,8080]
     }

B. For each line of the CSV (excluding the header) append a line to
   /home/user/devsecops/logs/compliance.log following this exact comma-separated format:

      service,port,status

   • status is OK if approved is yes and BLOCKED if approved is no.  
   • Preserve the row order found in services.csv—your log lines must appear in the same sequence as the CSV records.  
   • Do not include the CSV header in the log.  
   • A finished compliance.log therefore contains exactly five lines.

What the automated checker will look for
1. File /home/user/devsecops/policy_output/allowed_ports.json exists and is valid JSON.  
2. Inside that JSON, the allowed_ports array matches precisely the set of approved ports from the CSV, sorted ascending, with no duplicates.  
3. File /home/user/devsecops/logs/compliance.log exists and contains five lines whose content follows the specified “service,port,status” format and whose OK/BLOCKED decision matches the approved column of the CSV.

Hints
• Typical utilities that can help: awk or cut for CSV, sort, tr or sed for cleanup, jq for JSON creation (jq is installed).  
• Only 3-4 shell commands are sufficient if you think about streaming the data.  
• You do NOT need sudo rights. All paths are writable by the current user.  
• When you finish, you may display both output files to the console so a human can visually confirm your work.
truth:
Initial files/directories present before the agent starts:
1. Directory structure:
   /home/user/devsecops/
   /home/user/devsecops/policy_input/
   /home/user/devsecops/policy_output/
   /home/user/devsecops/logs/

2. File /home/user/devsecops/policy_input/services.csv with exact contents:
service,port,protocol,approved
ssh,22,tcp,yes
http,80,tcp,yes
telnet,23,tcp,no
smtp,25,tcp,yes
ftp,21,tcp,no

Expected artifacts AFTER successful completion:

A. /home/user/devsecops/policy_output/allowed_ports.json  
   File size: 43 bytes (including newline at end of file).  
   Exact byte-for-byte contents:
{
  "allowed_ports": [22,25,80]
}

(those are ASCII characters, newline at the very end)

B. /home/user/devsecops/logs/compliance.log  
   File size: 86 bytes (including newline after every line).  
   Exact byte-for-byte contents:
ssh,22,OK
http,80,OK
telnet,23,BLOCKED
smtp,25,OK
ftp,21,BLOCKED

(Each line ends with a single \n, including the last line)

The automated test will:
1. Verify that allowed_ports.json exists, is valid JSON, and that .allowed_ports equals the array [22,25,80] (numeric order).  
2. Verify that compliance.log exists and its five newline-terminated lines exactly match the list above.  
3. (Optionally) Confirm that no additional files appear inside /home/user/devsecops/policy_output/ or /home/user/devsecops/logs/.
